description: The provided visual is a non-scientific placeholder icon displaying a bookmark symbol and the phrase 'Check for updates' on a gray background, rather than an experimental materials-science figure. No scientific sub-panels, microscopy images, diffraction patterns, spectroscopic data, mechanical curves, axes, labels, scale bars, or any analytical features are visible or legible. The figure caption is empty and no citation sentences are provided. While the accompanying paper context describes a systematic investigation of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels containing 15–19 at.% Al—covering solution treatment, isothermal aging at 525–650 °C, microstructural evolution involving α → β-Mn transformation plus FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates, Vickers hardness, nanoindentation, and indentation fracture toughness—the specific data, comparisons, or conclusions intended for this figure remain unidentifiable from the unreadable placeholder image alone.

This schematic diagram illustrates the indentation-based methodology for evaluating fracture toughness in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels. Visually, it depicts a Vickers diamond indentation imprint with four radial cracks emanating from the four corners, where $l_i$ denotes the radial crack length measured from an indentation corner to the crack tip. The caption and citing text state that the average crack length $c$ is defined as $c = 0.25\sum_{i=1}^{4}l_i$, and is substituted into the indentation fracture toughness formula together with a Vickers load $P$ of 9.8 N, hardness HV, and elastic modulus $E$ measured from compressive stress-strain curves (164 GPa for 15Al, 175 GPa for 17Al, and 181 GPa for 19Al). The paper reports experimentally measured average crack lengths of 18 μm, 22 μm, and 35 μm for the peak-aged 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys, corresponding to calculated fracture toughness values of 8.6, 6.6, and 3.5 MPa·m^{1/2}, respectively. This figure establishes the geometric measurement protocol, supporting the core conclusion that increasing Al content progressively increases indentation crack propagation length and thereby degrades fracture toughness despite the alloys achieving ultra-high specific hardness.

Figure 2 presents the aging hardening responses of three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels—15Al, 17Al, and 19Al—showing Vickers hardness evolution as a function of aging time (0–10 h) at temperatures from 525 °C to 650 °C. For each alloy, the left panel displays hardness-time curves at six discrete aging temperatures, while the right panel provides a corresponding contour plot mapping hardness across the temperature-time parameter space. All alloys exhibit rapid hardness increases within the first hour, followed by plateau or slight over-aging behavior. The 15Al alloy achieves the highest peak hardness of approximately 966 HV at 600 °C for 6 h, whereas the 17Al and 19Al alloys peak at about 925 HV (575 °C/6 h) and 918 HV (600 °C/10 h), respectively. Notably, the optimal aging temperature window shifts to higher temperatures as Al content increases, and the 19Al alloy shows markedly slower hardening kinetics at lower aging temperatures compared with 15Al. These results demonstrate that increasing Al content retards the aging hardening response and reduces the peak hardness in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu steels, consistent with delayed β-Mn precipitation and transformation kinetics.

Figure 3 presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding phase maps for solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying aluminum contents. Subfigures (a), (b), and (c) display the as-quenched microstructures of the 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys (nominal Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu, at.%), respectively. The upper IPF maps reveal equiaxed BCC grains with random crystallographic orientations, while the lower phase maps demonstrate that all three solution-treated alloys consist of a single α (BCC) phase, indicated by the uniform red coloring with negligible γ (FCC) phase fraction. The scale bars denote 500 μm for (a) and (c), and 200 μm for (b). These results establish that the solid-solution condition yields a fully ferritic microstructure prior to aging, providing the baseline microstructural state from which subsequent aging-induced precipitates such as β-Mn and FCC Cu-rich phases form during thermal treatments at 525–650 °C.

Figure 4 displays EBSD inverse pole figures (IPFs) and corresponding phase maps for the 15Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel after different aging treatments. Panels a1 and a2 show the microstructure after aging at 525 °C for 3 h: the low-magnification IPF reveals fine grains, while the phase map (a1) and magnified view (a2) show predominantly β-Mn (green) with remaining untransformed α matrix (red, BCC); the caption reports that β-Mn reached 85.8 %. Panels b1 and b2 present the sample aged at 550 °C for 6 h, where the phase maps show the α matrix had almost entirely transformed into β-Mn, and the magnified phase map b2 highlights a new fine BCC phase (red) precipitated within β-Mn. Panels c1 and c2 correspond to aging at 650 °C for 3 h, showing significantly coarsened β-Mn grains in c1 and, in the magnified phase map c2, a new FCC phase (magenta) distributed within the β-Mn matrix with no untransformed α observed. Collectively, the figure demonstrates a sequential phase evolution from α + β-Mn co-existence toward nearly complete β-Mn dominance, accompanied by progressive secondary BCC and FCC precipitation as aging temperature increases, providing the microstructural origin of the rapid aging hardening behavior.

Figure 5 presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding phase maps for the 17Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel after aging at different temperatures. Panels (a1) and (a2) show the microstructure after aging at 525 °C for 10 h, where the low-magnification and magnified views reveal a dominant α matrix (BCC, red) with approximately 20.5 % β-Mn (green) precipitated along and within α grains, together with minor α-Mn (blue) particles. Panels (b1) and (b2) display the sample aged at 575 °C for 6 h, where the α matrix has almost completely transformed into β-Mn; the high-magnification phase map highlights tiny new FCC phase (pink) precipitates within the β-Mn matrix. Panels (c1) and (c2) correspond to aging at 650 °C for 10 h, showing nearly complete β-Mn transformation, with magnified EBSD revealing new BCC (red) and FCC (pink) precipitates dispersed in β-Mn. These results demonstrate that increasing aging temperature promotes the α → β-Mn phase transformation and leads to the formation of additional BCC and FCC precipitates within β-Mn at higher temperatures.

This figure presents paired EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding phase maps for the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu 19Al alloy under three isothermal aging conditions, capturing the temperature-driven microstructural evolution and phase transformation sequence. Subfigures a1 and a2 show the sample aged at 525 °C for 10 h, where coarse equiaxed α grains (BCC, red in the phase map) constitute the matrix with minor β-Mn (green) precipitated along grain boundaries and intragranularly; the magnified a2 and its phase map reveal dendritic β-Mn growing into the α matrix, and citation reports the β-Mn fraction is only 12.1 %. Subfigures b1 and b2 display the microstructure after aging at 600 °C for 3 h, exhibiting a much refined multiphase morphology with β-Mn as the dominant phase; the high-magnification b2 phase map identifies scattered new FCC phase particles (magenta) within the β-Mn matrix. Subfigures c1 and c2 depict the sample aged at 650 °C for 6 h, where the α matrix has almost completely transformed into β-Mn; the magnified c2 phase map clearly shows abundant new BCC phase (red) precipitates distributed within the β-Mn grains, consistent with the reported observation of substantial BCC formation at this condition. The overall trend demonstrates that elevating the aging temperature accelerates the α → β-Mn transformation and triggers secondary precipitation of FCC and BCC phases inside β-Mn. These results support the conclusion that increasing Al content to 19 at.% markedly slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics compared with lower-Al variants, while high-temperature aging drives near-complete β-Mn transformation accompanied by new BCC phase precipitation.

Figure 7 presents secondary electron (SE) images showing the microstructural evolution of the 15Al alloy (Fe-Mn-Al-Cu) after isothermal aging at 525–650 °C for 1–10 h. The 16 panels collectively document the precipitation sequence and phase transformation from the α matrix to β-Mn. At 525 °C, fine α-Mn particles appear after 1 h, while short rod-shaped β-Mn precipitates along α grain boundaries and interiors become visible by 3 h alongside untransformed α; by 6 h the microstructure is dominated by β-Mn, and new precipitates emerge after 10 h. Similar trends at 550 °C and 575 °C show rapid β-Mn formation within 1–3 h, with complete transformation and subsequent new precipitation at longer times. At higher temperatures (600–625 °C), β-Mn forms even after 1 h, whereas at 650 °C/1 h new precipitates appear immediately. These micrographs demonstrate that the 15Al alloy exhibits fast β-Mn precipitation kinetics, with the α matrix almost fully transformed within 3 h at 525 °C, supporting the conclusion that ultrahigh hardness originates from extensive β-Mn formation.

This figure presents sixteen secondary electron microscopy (SEM) images of the 17Al alloy (Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel) subjected to isothermal aging at 525–650 °C for durations of 1–10 h, capturing the temperature- and time-dependent microstructural evolution. At 525 °C, β-Mn precipitates nucleate along grain boundaries and grow progressively with aging time, while α-Mn particles become visible by 6 h (shown in the inset). At 550 °C, the α matrix initially coexists with β-Mn at 1 h (inset confirms α-Mn), after which β-Mn dominates the microstructure at longer times. Upon aging at 575 °C and 600 °C, new fine precipitates emerge within the matrix after 6–10 h and at 1 h (600 °C), respectively, indicating a secondary precipitation stage. The higher-temperature conditions (625 °C and 650 °C for 1 h) likewise reveal the formation of new precipitates. Collectively, the images document a clear transition from intergranular β-Mn precipitation at lower temperatures toward extensive β-Mn coverage and subsequent intragranular precipitation at intermediate temperatures, providing microstructural evidence for the multi-stage phase transformations underlying the alloy’s aging hardening behavior.

Figure 9 presents secondary electron microscopy (SEM) images showing the microstructural evolution of the 19Al alloy subjected to isothermal aging at 525–650 °C for 1–10 h. The 16 sub-panels systematically document phase transformations with increasing temperature and holding time. At 525 °C, fine α-Mn particles initially precipitate within the matrix (1 h, 6 h), while β-Mn forms along grain boundaries and interiors (6 h, 10 h). At 550 °C, untransformed α regions persist at shorter durations (3 h, 6 h), but β-Mn becomes dominant at 10 h with additional new precipitates. At 575–600 °C, the β-Mn transformation proceeds rapidly, with untransformed α visible at 3 h and new precipitates emerging at 6–10 h. At 625–650 °C, aging for 1 h yields a relatively homogeneous matrix with evidence of new precipitate formation. The figure demonstrates that elevated aging temperatures and prolonged times accelerate the α → β-Mn transformation and subsequent precipitation of secondary phases, supporting the identification of optimal aging windows for ultra-high hardness in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels.

Figure 10 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying Al contents (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al at.%) subjected to selected aging treatments. The black curve shows the 15Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 1 h, the red curve shows the 17Al alloy aged at 575 °C for 1 h, and the blue curve shows the 19Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 6 h. Indexed diffraction peaks indicate the presence of α/D0₃ (diamonds), α-Mn (flowers), and β-Mn (triangles) phases across the 2θ range of 20° to 110°. The patterns reveal that both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks coexist in these selected conditions, indicating coprecipitation of α-Mn and β-Mn during aging prior to complete transformation of the α matrix to β-Mn. The 19Al alloy exhibits particularly prominent β-Mn peaks after prolonged aging, while the relative intensities and presence of α/D0₃ reflections vary with composition and aging parameters. These results support the conclusion that increasing Al content slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics and that both α-Mn and β-Mn phases precipitate simultaneously during the early-to-mid stages of aging in these ultra-high specific hardness steels.

TEM micrographs of the 15Al alloy (Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu) aged at 600 °C for 6 h, revealing complete disappearance of the α matrix and formation of abundant near-equiaxed β-Mn grains. Panel (a) presents low-magnification bright-field and corresponding dark-field images showing fine particles uniformly distributed at β-Mn interfaces. Panel (b) displays selected area electron diffraction patterns: the green-circled region confirms the β-Mn phase along the [012] zone axis, while the orange and blue circled areas demonstrate that the interfacial fine particles possess an FCC crystalline structure containing FCC twins and correspond to a Cu-rich FCC phase along the [011] beam direction. Panel (c) provides a bright-field image alongside EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu, indicating the FCC phase is enriched in Cu, depleted in Fe and Mn, and slightly poor in Al, whereas β-Mn is poor in Cu. These observations support that peak aging produces a microstructure dominated by β-Mn with Cu-rich FCC precipitates at its interfaces, contributing to the ultra-high specific hardness.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM characterization of the 17Al alloy (Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu) aged at 575 °C for 6 h (peak-hardness condition). Panel (a) shows low-magnification bright-field (BF) images of the overall microstructure. Panel (b) provides a high-magnification BF image alongside EDS elemental maps (Fe, Mn, Al, Cu) revealing Cu enrichment in precipitates, together with a selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern from the blue-circled region identifying FCC twins along the [011] beam direction. Panel (c) displays BF images of β-Mn regions with corresponding SAED patterns indexed along the [111] beam confirming the β-Mn phase, and a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image with FFT verifying the β-Mn crystal structure. Panel (d) shows an HRTEM image of the interface between the FCC Cu-rich phase and β-Mn, with FFT insets from both sides clearly distinguishing the two phases. Collectively, these results demonstrate that the peak-aged microstructure is dominated by β-Mn with fine FCC Cu-rich particles (exhibiting twins) at β-Mn interfaces, providing direct structural evidence for the alloy's ultrahigh specific hardness.

Figure 13 presents nanoindentation results for peak-aged Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying Al contents: 15Al aged at 600 °C for 6 h, 17Al aged at 575 °C for 6 h, and 19Al aged at 600 °C for 10 h. Panel (a) shows load–displacement curves for the three alloys, reporting average nano-hardness values of 11.7 ± 0.4 GPa, 11.1 ± 0.6 GPa, and 10.4 ± 1.3 GPa, respectively, where both the mean hardness and curve-to-curve variability trend downward with increasing Al. The accompanying secondary electron (SE) micrographs display arrays of nanoindentation impressions on 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al, each annotated with local hardness readings and a 2 μm scale bar. The individual indent hardnesses span roughly 10.9–12.4 GPa for 15Al, 10.2–12.3 GPa for 17Al, and 8.0–12.9 GPa for 19Al, revealing progressively broader distributions as Al rises. This increasing scatter reflects the greater volume fraction of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates formed between β-Mn interfaces at higher Al contents. Collectively, the data support the conclusion that although elevated Al marginally raises the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn through solid-solution strengthening, the overall micro-hardness decreases with increasing Al because the growing proportion of softer interfacial precipitates reduces the absolute β-Mn volume fraction and introduces larger property fluctuations.

This bar chart presents SEM-EDS quantitative compositional data for the β-Mn phase in the peak-hardness (hardest) samples of three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels designated 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al, represented by red, blue, and yellow bars respectively. The x-axis identifies the measured elements Mn, Al, and Cu, while the y-axis indicates atomic percentage. The Mn content remains consistently high and nearly unchanged across all three alloys at approximately 36.2–36.8 at.%, and Cu remains low at roughly 2.6–2.8 at.%. In contrast, the Al content within β-Mn increases systematically with nominal alloy aluminum, measuring 14.3 at.% for 15Al, 16.3 at.% for 17Al, and 17.5 at.% for 19Al. These results show that higher Al additions raise Al solubility in β-Mn, providing slight solid-solution strengthening that increases the intrinsic nanoindentation hardness of β-Mn from about 12.1 GPa to 12.5 GPa. Nevertheless, the overall bulk hardness of the alloys decreases with increasing Al content because the fraction of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates forming at β-Mn interfaces progressively grows, offsetting the strengthening benefit.

This figure presents combined EBSD and EDS characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying Al contents (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al at.%) aged at 525 °C for 3 h (15Al) or 10 h (17Al and 19Al). Each row comprises four columns: FSD images revealing surface morphology and phase contrast; IPF maps showing grain orientations; phase maps distinguishing β-Mn (green), BCC (red), α-Mn (blue), and FCC (magenta); and EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu. The 15Al alloy exhibits extensive β-Mn formation alongside residual BCC matrix after only 3 h, whereas the 17Al and 19Al alloys display progressively reduced β-Mn precipitation after 10 h, consistent with retarded transformation kinetics at higher Al contents. EDS mapping confirms that β-Mn is Mn-rich but depleted in Al and Cu, while Cu segregates to discrete FCC regions. These observations demonstrate that increasing Al content suppresses the α→β-Mn phase transformation rate and governs elemental partitioning during aging.

This figure presents a comparative scatter plot of specific hardness (hardness divided by density, HV·cm³/g) versus density (g/cm³) for the developed Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels against various reference material classes. The x-axis spans 6.0–9.0 g/cm³ and the y-axis spans 0–160 HV·cm³/g. Three alloy variants from this study—15Al (blue filled circles), 17Al (green filled circles), and 19Al (red filled circles)—are prominently clustered at low densities (~6.6–6.9 g/cm³) with ultra-high specific hardness values exceeding 120 HV·cm³/g and reaching above 140 HV·cm³/g. These clusters sit well above a dashed reference line near 120 HV·cm³/g. The background includes comparative data clouds for conventional low-density steels (gray-shaded region with orange and blue open circles), other steels including stainless and carbon steels (pink open squares, ~7.3–8.0 g/cm³), pure iron (black triangle near 7.8 g/cm³), and medium/high-entropy alloys M/HEAs (blue open hexagons, ~7.5–8.8 g/cm³). The plot visually establishes that the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu alloys simultaneously achieve lower density and markedly higher specific hardness than existing steels and M/HEAs, supporting the core conclusion that these alloys overcome the specific hardness ceiling of conventional high-performance steels.
